narrative:
Let's take a closer look at the prediction that your application for university admission was rejected because the model predicted you would fail the bar exam. This prediction is based on several factors from your academic and personal background. Currently, the model estimates a 65.9% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This is primarily due to certain academic indicators and scores that are below the average of other students who have successfully passed.

To help you understand what could change this prediction, we use something called counterfactual scenarios. These are "what if" situations that show how altering certain aspects of your profile could lead to a different outcome, specifically predicting that you would pass the exam. In your case, the model considered several scenarios where small changes in your academic performance could flip the prediction to a more favorable one.

From the analysis, it appears that your undergraduate GPA (ugpa) and LSAT score are crucial factors. In all scenarios where the prediction changed to passing, your GPA needed to increase, sometimes alongside a slight improvement in your LSAT score. Interestingly, your family income, gender, racial background, and employment status did not need to change in any scenario, indicating that these factors are less influential in altering the prediction.

In total, three counterfactual scenarios were generated, and in each, your GPA was a consistent factor that needed improvement. In some cases, a small increase in your LSAT score also contributed to a positive change. This suggests that focusing on improving your academic performance, particularly your GPA, could significantly impact the prediction. 

To make these changes actionable, consider strategies to enhance your study habits and seek academic support to boost your GPA. Even a modest increase in your GPA, closer to the average of 3.21, could make a substantial difference. Additionally, if feasible, aim to slightly improve your LSAT score, as even a one-point increase was shown to be beneficial in some scenarios. By focusing on these areas, you can work towards a more favorable prediction and potentially have your application reconsidered.
